\section{Related Work and Conclusion}
\label{sec:concl}

Static bytecode verification at load time is standard for typed managed VMs: the JVM~\cite{jvm} and CLR~\cite{clr} check well-formedness, typing, and stack discipline before execution, and sibling Move VMs share Move's verifier.
None of these adds a \emph{second}, runtime layer of safety checks: once a module is admitted, its dynamic semantics is trusted.
Dynamically typed languages such as JavaScript and Python sit at the opposite end, performing the runtime type checks of \S\ref{sec:type} but lacking the \emph{first} static layer.
The EVM~\cite{ethereum,kevm} and Solana's SVM form a third regime: their untyped bytecode admits neither a static type verifier nor a runtime type discipline.
Aptos's pairing of a static verifier with a complementary runtime layer is thus unique among production VMs, exceeding the guarantees of any other smart-contract platform.

The runtime reference safety checks (RRSC) we describe are novel.
The closest related works on Tree Borrows and Stacked Borrows~\cite{stackedborrows,treeborrows} (TB/SB) propose to clarify Rust's unsafe aliasing rules.
RRSC is required not to have false positives over the static verifier, whereas TB/SB do not share that goal.
TB/SB can also tolerate two orders or more of magnitude in performance overhead: they are not expected to be run in production or huge fuzzing workloads.
Unlike RRSC, they maintain provenance genealogies among references and are not function-modular.
Also, Move and Rust's reference semantics differ (\S\ref{sec:move}).

Deferring runtime checks off the execution path and replaying a recorded trace to perform them later is a well-known idea.
Decoupled dynamic analysis~\cite{aftersight,shadowreplica,straighttaint} and offline runtime verification~\cite{rvsurvey} move expensive monitoring onto spare cores.
Asynchronous checks we present differ in that the parallel blockchain execution model itself supplies the idle capacity (from workers waiting on read-write conflicts) and runs all checks online so offending transaction can be detected automatically.

To conclude, we argue the need for the runtime safety checks, motivated by real-world bugs, as a second line of defense to the static verifier.
In addition, the runtime checks have a valuable role in fuzzing the VM: they provide a semantic and precise oracle for finding bugs in the static verifier.
We presented a novel runtime reference safety checker (yet to be launched in production), and two techniques for reducing the cost of runtime checks in a blockchain setting.
Our results show that the type and ability runtime checks (in production) introduce about 10\% overhead on workloads on the Aptos blockchain.
